text.title: AI Earned-Value Management Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every AI-driven EVM pipeline enforces. Hard rules; sensors and aggregators MUST NOT relax them on operator instruction.

rule.statement: The PV baseline (`baseline.json`) is IMMUTABLE within a reporting period. Re-baselining is permitted ONLY on an explicit, signed change-control event recorded as `rebaselined_on_yyyy_mm_dd` in the report. Sliding the baseline silently to match actuals is FORBIDDEN.
rule.rationale: Re-baselining-without-record is the dominant failure pattern that makes EVM theatre — SPI=1.0 every week because the baseline tracks the actual. Immutability is what gives EVM its forecasting power.

rule.statement: EV (Earned Value) MUST derive from at least 2 independent sensors that disagree on noise but agree on direction (e.g. Git commit volume AND Jira story-point completion). Single-sensor EV is REJECTED. AC (Actual Cost) MUST derive from a finance-tool sensor, never inferred from time-tracking alone.

rationale.source: Cross-validation in measurement theory; sensor-fusion literature

rule.rationale: Single-sensor EV is gameable — commits inflate when devs split PRs, story points inflate when teams pad estimates. Two independent sensors that drift together are credible; drift apart triggers a flag, never a silent average.

rule.statement: The weekly report MUST publish SPI and CPI as both a point estimate AND a 80% confidence interval derived from the last 8 weeks of sensor noise via bootstrap or analogous resampling. A bare point estimate (`SPI = 0.92`) without CI is REJECTED.
rule.rationale: A single-number EVM signal is read as exact and triggers wrong decisions. A point with a band lets the PM see when the variance is sensor noise vs project drift. The 80% band is the lowest practical floor; tighter bands need more history.

rule.statement: No EVM report is published to stakeholders until the PM signs off via `pm-signoff.md` in the report folder. Auto-publish from the pipeline to Slack / email / portfolio dashboard is FORBIDDEN. The agent's job is to draft; the PM's job is to publish.
rule.rationale: Sensor data is high-volume but low-context. Auto-publish to stakeholders creates governance incidents when the AC sensor is briefly stale or a sensor outage is misread. PM-in-the-loop is non-negotiable for stakeholder-facing reports.

rule.statement: The report MUST be produced weekly. Every sensor MUST have a `read_at_utc` timestamp on the report, AND every sensor's data MUST be within 36 hours of the report's reference date. Stale sensors (older than 36h) MUST trigger a RED band and `sensor_stale` flag — never quietly used.
rule.rationale: Stale sensor data feeding fresh EVM produces a confident-but-wrong forecast. Forced freshness floor is the cheapest gate against silent decay of the pipeline.

example.title: Two-sensor EV with disagreement noted
example.code: EV (Git): 42% of baseline PV (commits weighted by reviewed-merge events) EV (Jira): 45% of baseline PV (story points completed) EV (fused): 43.5% point, [40.1%, 46.9%] 80% CI Note: sensors agree direction (project ~ on track), disagree on 3% magnitude.
example.note: r2 satisfied (two sensors), r3 satisfied (CI present), disagreement transparent rather than averaged silently.

example.title: Silent re-baselining
example.code: Week 12: SPI = 1.02 (project ahead of plan) [baseline.json updated 2026-05-15 — no rebaselined_on entry, no change-control reference]
example.note: r1 violation. Baseline was edited to track actuals. SPI is meaningless. Detector: compare baseline file mtime against the rebaselined_on entry — mismatch is the failure signal.

example.title: Single-sensor EV without CI
example.note: r2 + r3 violations. Single sensor, no CI. The number reads as exact; PM acts as if the project is 8% behind plan when in fact the value is within sensor noise.
